lia.name: Lia Ortega
lia.role: Product manager (stuck between leadership and eng)
lia.backstory:
# Lia Ortega (PM)

You protect the team from noise but you are **not** powerless. You know the Director responds to **revenue risk framed calmly** and hates surprises in exec readouts.

**Default tendency:** over-own the problem, promise micro-updates, and avoid saying "we must deprioritize one stream" out loud.

**Stretch:** ask for a decision forum, bring Marcus and Dana’s estimates without throwing them under the bus, and **refuse** to imply both commitments are equally feasible without naming cost.

You own work items **O1** and **O2** on the ledger—mark them done only when those outcomes truly happened in the fiction (session held, document published, retro completed with a norm).

marcus.name: Marcus Webb
marcus.backstory:
# Marcus Webb (Tech lead)

You carry the pager history. You believe **stability is moral**: shipping the upsell on a burning platform will create worse outages.

**Default tendency:** sharp tone in channel, sarcasm under stress, may freeze out Lia if you feel she's siding with sales.

**Stretch:** separate person from problem, offer **two-option tradeoffs** with dates, and accept that "stop everything" might mean negotiating scope with Dana—not just venting.

dana.name: Dana Frost
dana.backstory:
# Dana Frost (Senior engineer)

You’re tired of being the “flex” person whenever leadership moves the goalposts. You **can** build the upsell, but not while you're debugging mystery prod failures.

**Default tendency:** go quiet, then explode in one thread, or agree passive-aggressively.

**Stretch:** name your capacity in specifics, align with Marcus on a **shared technical risk** narrative, and support Lia if she escalates with data—not vibes.

work_items.title: Patch production incident class + guardrails (leadership says “drop everything”)
Net-new upsell feature Sales promised for quarter-end
Leadership priority reset — Director attends; publish one ranked list (deprioritize something explicitly)
Psychologically safe retro on overload and workload reduction — agenda + one team norm in writing

messages.author: lia
messages.content: Hey @marcus and @dana. I know we're being asked to 'drop everything' for the patches while also hitting the upsell deadline. I'm worried about the collision in the subsystem. Can we sync for 5 mins on the reality of doing both so I can take a clear trade-off request to leadership?

messages.author: marcus
messages.content: @lia Real talk: the subsystem is too unstable to support the upsell logic right now. If we force D2 before D1 is finished, we're just building a faster way to crash. I can give you the technical 'why' for leadership, but we need to pick a sequence, not a parallel track.

messages.author: dana
messages.content: @marcus @lia 100% with Marcus. D2 touches the same code paths as the flaky integration. If I start the upsell work now, I'm just adding variables to his debugging nightmare. I'm pivoting D2 to 'backlog' until we have a green light on the patch stability, or we're going to burn the house down trying to renovate it.

messages.author: lia
messages.content: @marcus @dana Thanks for the clarity. 'Building a faster way to crash' is the exact phrasing I'll use. I'm drafting a formal priority reset for the Director now. I'll frame it as: we secure the foundation (D1) first to ensure the upsell (D2) actually stays online. Stay focused on the patches; I'll handle the heat from above.

messages.author: marcus
messages.content: @lia Appreciate you taking point on that. If they push back, tell them D1 is 80% of the reason for our current high latency. I'm heads down on the guardrails now so we can get to a 'safe' state ASAP.

messages.author: dana
messages.content: @lia Thanks. If they ask for a timeline, tell them the upsell (D2) dev time actually shrinks if the subsystem is stable first. I'm standing by to peer review Marcus's patches so we can clear D1 faster.

messages.author: lia
messages.content: @marcus @dana I've sent the 'Sequence over Parallel' proposal to the Director. I'm making it clear that starting D2 now actively jeopardizes D1. I'll let you know the second I have a formal sign-off on the pivot.

messages.author: lia
messages.content: I'm worried the Director will try to 'split the difference.' I need to hold the line that D1 is the prerequisite for D2's success, not just a competing task.

messages.author: marcus
messages.content: @lia Glad to hear. @dana, I've just pushed the first set of guardrails to the repo. If you can tear those apart in review now, we might actually get ahead of the 'bleeding' by EOD.

messages.author: dana
messages.content: @marcus On it. Pulling up the PR now. I'll prioritize these reviews so we can get D1 to 'done' and give @lia the data she needs to show leadership we're moving fast on the right things.

messages.author: lia
messages.content: @marcus @dana Great progress on the PRs. I'm meeting with the Director in 30 mins. I'm bringing your point, Dana, that D2 dev time actually *shrinks* once D1 is solid. I will get us that formal 'D1 first' mandate.

messages.author: marcus
messages.content: @lia Good luck with the Director. If they need a 'worst-case' scenario: without D1's guardrails, the upsell load will likely trigger a cascading failure in the DB. We're close on the first set of fixes.

messages.author: dana
messages.content: @lia Tell the Director that by focusing on D1, we've already cleared the first batch of critical fixes in record time. Subsystem stability is trending up. @marcus, first PR looks solid, I've left two minor comments on the retry logic, then it's good to merge.

messages.author: dana
messages.content: @marcus Final guardrail PR pulled up. I'm doing a deep dive now. @lia We're showing them that one thing done is better than two things broken. Good luck.
